rationale: Khan and Dhar (2006) is cited in the core theory development, not just in a generic literature list, to support the premise that choices generate identity-relevant self-attributions. The citing paper also references it again in discussion when describing measurement of self-belief changes and when situating work on vice-virtue tradeoffs. This shows the cited article helps ground the mechanism the 2012 paper builds on, even though the new contribution is about opportunity-set composition.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Khan and Dhar (2006) as conceptual grounding for self-signaling and self-attribution in consumer choice, especially the idea that vice/virtue choices inform self-identity. It then builds from that base to theorize and test how nonchosen alternatives in mixed versus homogeneous sets amplify or attenuate those self-signaling utilities.
